comment_text: Save yourself 5 grand and get a 2021 - 2024 flexstay stumpy

I’ll second this. My 2021 flex-stay SJ is still going strong after 5 seasons. It’s light weight and ideal for long trail rides that may include a few sections of gnarly terrain. I’m on my 3rd enduro bike over the same period but the stumpy just keeps going. Recently upgraded the fox dps to a float x andnot only is it a bit more capable, it pedals better as well. I also have the mullet link* which makes it super versatile - great for riding flow trails. *it’s not well known but there is a mullet link available for the non-evo SJ

@bikingduker : I replaced my Izzo (cracked frame they wouldn't warranty) with a flex-stay SJ frame and moved all the parts over. I've got a beefed up Stumpy Evo for enduro/park and a Turbo Levo. The flex-stay SJ is the bike I reach for the most when I'm going on a ride (admittedly, I live in Northwest Arkansas, so the other two are a bit of overkill). It's a really underrated bike, imo.

I'm still so confused by Specialized's decision to remerge the stumpy and evo. Both bikes were loved and instead of continuing down that path, they created a bike in the middle that does everything worse.
Really? What kind of total build weights are you getting? I've seen high 20s at best.

its what's most interesting to me these days - a lot of non-ebikes existed before and the new replacements only have better brakes, suspension. a used frame can be just as good, or better. Bikes havent magically evolved since 2020.. and in fact, one could get get a santacruz hightower 1 (2016/7?) and run it as a downcountry bike these days.
@GlazedHam : you can get 28lbs pretty easily with carbon wheels and lighter tires. Mine is an XL with stock alloy wheels, butcher/eliminator tires, piggyback shock and its just over 30lbs
Shameless plug mine is for sale right now! A few parts upgrades to make it more dh capable and weighs 29lbs no pedals.

Overpaid for mine in 2022, and it's amazing. I'm sticking with it as long as I can. Currently aiming to run it to at least 2032.

@PHeller : Yes, they do. That platform is anomalously light - no flex-stay, frame storage, reputedly stiff and 150mm-capable. That asymmetrical brace must save more weight than you'd think.

Imagine spending hundreds of thousands of dollars bringing a bike like this to market, only to have the most unimaginative, droll, unenthusiastic potato of a bike reviewer review it. Literally every bike review ends the same way: "This bike is fine, but nothing really sets it apart from all the other bikes, so. just get whatever you want." Dude it's literally you're job to MAKE PEOPLE EXCITED ABOUT A BIKE. Yes, most bikes are super similar these days, but as editor of pinkbike (this is a hot take) you should have a sufficient level of bike autism to talk about unique attributes of bikes, no matter how similar they are.

@kashimadaddy : Allied didn't give him much to work with. It's not made in the USA anymore. It's light, but not the only light trail bike on the market. It's expensive, with no outstanding qualities. Let the brands build their own excitement but I don't think the journalists need to hype a bike that doesn't deserve it.
@kashimadaddy : It's marketing's job to make people excited about a bike. It's a reviewer's job to review a bike.
@kashimadaddy : Honesty excites some of us. No need to hype up stuff for the sake of hyping.
@PHeller : their font spoiled it for me as well

@sjk303 : It's a reviewers job to point out the unique attributes of the item being reviewed that might excite eventual buyers/customers of the item in question, or to point out flaws. Do they need to be genuinely excited about every bike or pretend it's incredible? No, but there's something unique about almost every bike that a buyer could get excited about, even if it's intangible in nature. Similar thing with the Norco Revolver. I was super interested in that bike and waited months for a review. Imagine my disappointment when I found out Mike reviewed it, as his takeaway was, "Yeah, it's an XC bike with flexstays, not much unique about it from other XC bikes with flex stays." What a wild thing to say when it's literally your job to fuss over bikes.

@kashimadaddy : Agreed. Kaz never has anything to say interesting about any bike these days. Like, sure maybe the bike doesn't stand out from the crowd, but at least write in a way that is interesting for the reader and gets us engaged with you. This hum-drum writing is probably why most people on PB just skip to the comments: Why even bother reading this stuff?
If I lent Dangerholm my Status for the weekend he'd return it to me weighing under 27 lbs

@kashimadaddy : I think you're mixing up the role of a paid influencer with an unpaid journalist. There are bikes that excite Kazimer, this just isn't one of them. Influencers are paid by marketing teams to hype new products up.

Like several commenters mentioned, it’s not my job to manufacture hype. If a bike gets me excited that’ll be clearly evident in the review. I’m lucky enough to ride dozens of bikes every year, so I have a good baseline for comparisons, and I try to lay out the strengths and weakness of each bike in a way that helps readers start to decide whether or not that bike is worth their consideration. Some bikes shine brighter than others, and sharing those findings is the whole point of a review in the first place.

@mooseindahouse : Travel definitely isn't everything and I don't doubt Kaz's take. I really appreciate his objectivity and honesty. However, when comparing a 120mm flex stay bike against a 140mm horst link bike (with slacker geometry), it's hard for me to understand how the extra inch of travel, plus the more descending-biased geometry, plus the more sensative suspension design wouldn't make the latter bike substantially more capable. Clearly there's something intangible going on, but its not clear what it is. I'm dissapointed becuase this bike seems excellent on paper. Not just for "Arkansas riding," as the review suggests, but for a lot of places that aren't the PNW -- where you need to spin a long ways over varied terrain to get to the best descents.

@angryhiker : How friendly do you think Allied is to PB vs other brands, like say, Transition? You are right, more aggressive geo and an extra inch with Horst link doesn't add up. Is the intangible part all the Walton FU money? I love the new Spur too, but it seems like Allied build something unique at this weight instead of building an enduro frame with XC parts.
Genius, last gen stumpy, smuggler, top fuel, trail cat, occam so come to mind as bikes in the 140/140 or 140/130 category with equally light weight

@kashimadaddy : maybe there isn’t something interesting about every new bike out there? It’s all a pretty flat soup of flexstay XC bikes, Horst link trail bikes and heavy enduro bikes these days, all with similar geo and similar kinematics because these have mostly been figured out by now.

My apologies in advance for commenting on a product I actually own and ride. I've had the Fitz set up with a 140mm with a Fox36SL, and it does in fact feel like what a trail bike should be. We shouldn't overcomplicate it by talking about 120mm bikes and the fact that they feel livelier, just like we shouldn't compare it to 160mm enduro sleds. It's a trail bike that's actually a trail bike, and I'm thankful for that! I've owned many similar bikes on paper (Flex Stay stumpy, 140mm Rocky Mountain Instinct before they got super heavy, and many others). Also owned a Spur, which I loved deeply, and too many others over the years. The Fitz is much more versatile than most bikes out there. The fact that you can build it up to 26 pounds, which is in line with an XC spur build, is crazy to me. Sure, the Spur bats above its league, but why not have superior suspension for rougher terrain without a weight penalty! I currently have my Fitz setup with a 150mm Fox Neo rear shock and the new Lyrik 150mm, 2.5" Radial tires, sub 30 pounds in beast mode, and it slays! Definitely do not compare mine to a bowl of oatmeal, what the heck.
No need to apologize, commenting about your real world experiences is exactly what the comments are for. Sounds like a sweet build - glad you’re enjoying it!

I personally can't tell too much of a difference between a 120SID and a 130-140 Pike effectively speaking- trails that overwhelm the 120 still overwhelm the Pike at 130 and 140, at least in my experience. The bigger difference is in the rear sus, you can actually stand and pedal a 120 bike, feels snappy, but a 140 bike is like running in place on a 30 year old sofa. 90% of the capability is the tire choice at these travel numbers. Suffice to say, I get why Kaz might say a snappier shorter travel bike is a better tool for the same trails as this bike.
Pike seems more durable. My SiD gave up after 250 km in my Transition Spur riding a bit tech-ier stuff in my front doors...

I love my BC40 as an XC bike but wanted a more compliant daily driver for the trails in Utah. I have a handful of rides on the Fitz and would agree with Mike's experience. First thing I did was move the Motives to my BC40 (replacing Levels) and put Mavens on the Fitz. Is it overkill? Maybe. Does it increase the weight by more than half a pound? Definitely, but it gives me much more confidence on steep and fast terrain. Overall, I've enjoyed the Fitz and think it's right at home on the types of trails Mike describes. Still want to play with the rear suspension settings as I'm getting too much pedal bob during hard pedaling if I don't move the switch to firm. Not sure if this is an air pressure adjustment or more low speed compression.
Not overkill at all. IMO, Mavens should be on every bike with more than 130mm of travel.
Tell me more about your BC40 as an XC bike...positives and negatives. I might be getting a new XC bike this year.

@needmoregears : No complaints, strong promoter. Have found it to be well balanced - climbs super well and stable going down. Obviously, if it's super chunky, you'll get bounced around a bit just given the weight and travel. I built mine with Flight Attendant, which I'm a fan of for XC racing. My biggest complaint has been the Level Ultimate brakes as I found that I was bleeding them every 10-15 hours of riding given the longer descents of 2K+ FT. The Motives so far have seemed like an upgrade. Seat tube insertion depth is on the shorter side given the kink the seat tube, so I'm running a 150 on a size medium. Went from a 50 to 60 mm stem as the medium felt a bit cramped early on. From a brand perspective, have found the folks at Allied to be super cool and willing to work with you on spec builts, color choices, etc. You're definitely paying more for that level of customization.

Kind of crazy how (if you buy into the objectivity of these reviews) on-paper metrics like weight and travel don’t seem to matter. 140mm 26lb Fitz? Mediocre descender, less than energetic climber 120mm 27-28lb spur 2.0? Better climber and (maybe) a more consistent descender than the fitz(?) 125mm Gen 2 Norco optic with steeper geo than either of the above bikes? Literally feels like an enduro sled going downhill.

It's almost like static geometry numbers on a chart change when everything sags in. Some bikes sit into the travel more at the same shock sag, meaning that steeper topped out head angle rides slacker on the trail.
I got a chance to ride a Fitz. We are in Durango Colorado so lots flowy climbing but long descents. My experience as that this bike climbs just as good as my Epic 8 Evo and descends way better.

Looks like a big fat e-bike. I don't understand why try to hide the shock on a bike like this, especially if it results in having flimsy plastic covers (and no its not the same as having a simple rubber protector on the downtube).

Biased as someone who owns this bike, but I think it's really less about keeping the shock in the frame and more about getting the kinematics right without worrying about shock placement. If the shock had to be outside the frame it's a bit harder to get the kinematics just the way you want it, but by being OK with it being inside the frame you have more freedom with shock placement. Similar to how the current Cannondale jekyl has the split downtube to get the shock placement where they want it. Plus it's a "cool feature" they can use for marketing, and leaves the inside of the front triangle open so you can fit a water bottle in it (mine came with removable bottle cage mounts)

I’ve been mostly against e-bikes since the start. To me, putting a motor on a bicycle is not just an advancement of bicycle tech the way a drivetrain, suspension, or a dropper post are. None of these inherently add power to the bike that doesn’t come from me. In fact they all made the bike heavier and require more power from me to move around but I like the benefit so the trade off makes sense. Never thought of myself as a purest but I guess in that sense I am, a human power purist. That’s the magic of a bicycle to me. Of course going for a run/walk/hike my total system weight is lighter, but adding the weight of a bicycle actually makes most travel easier despite the additional weight. I know it’s just simple science and physics but it’s a marvel, almost like magic.

I have this bike, with 36 factory and XT di2. I've had it for a few months and given it a good time. My observations are, the motor is more than enough for slower technical climbs. It's only when riding with friends on full-power on access roads and wider open climbs that you notice the lower power. I have gone on 4hr+ rides and still had plenty of batter left. I find it rides very well through chunderry technical descents, it does well in holding a straight line and charging quite hard for what it is. It's quite predictable. I find it very boring in mellow flowy XC trails. The 25kph cut-off does my head in. It does well in feeling more like a regular bike than the heavier ones. All in, I'm happy with the purchase. It doesn't have the same flickability as my trail bike but it goes harder. The adjustability sounds great and was a selling point for me, but in reality, when you want to go from the stock 150mm fork to a 170mm fork to go LX, it means a new fork now that there is no 170mm air spring for the 36. That's the real let down. It's not a air spring change and a rocker, it's a significant cost. So it just stays in the config you bought it. And I thought the removable battery would be cool for chairlift days. Just remove the battery and run it like a normal bike, but the derailleur only works when powered on as it's wired to the battery.

I've owned the Fuel EXE for 2 years and ridden it on all kinds of terrain here in Australia, from fast smooth flow trails, jumps and very steep tech. I love the thing, its such a fun and capable bike. I spent several days on the new Fuel+ in the MX setup in Squamish mid last year and it was a noticeable improvement overall. The power delivery felt nicer, the increase in torque was great and one of the biggest positives to me is the increase in battery size and therefore range, which is the main issue I have with the EXE. I will often run a range extender at our local zone to keep up with my friends on their Slash+ and get more than a few laps in. The suspension platform was the other highlight for me, which felt playful and bottomless compared to the EXE which I have tried in a variety of setups including 150/140mm, 160/147mm and 160/152mm. The Fox suspension combo was fantastic for all kinds of trails, I rode everything I would normally ride on my long travel enduro Spire/Dreadnought and I was immediately comfortable on the bike. The fact that I could do massive days without worrying about being stranded was a bonus! Then the ability to have several different setups is a big drawcard for me and I will be ordering one as soon as I can. For comparison I have ridden the Slash+, tried 2 different offerings from Transition among many other e-bikes and I keep coming back to the Fuel+ for top choice as my next e-bike.

I would also add that I have ridden a number of full power e-bikes and something running each different kind of motor including the Amflow and my personal preference is still the SL platform. They feel far more natural, and the TQ is wonderfully quiet. Sure SL's aren't for everyone but having a bunch of power/weight takes too much away from the handling and enjoyment of riding for me.

@74NZ : Well, technically, VPP is just another type of four-bar suspension linkage. There are four members/beams determining the articulation (axle path) of the rear wheel, just like on a Horst-Link layout. Any Horst-Link is also technically a "VPP", because they also don't have a fixed main pivot, but a virtual pivoting point. All that is to say; The term "VPP", as used by Santa Cruz, has never been anything more than a cleverly utilized marketing buzzword. The "VPP"-layout was originally created by Santa Cruz to achieve similar suspension kinematics to Specializeds' Horst-Link setup, but sidestep their patent protection.

I for one think its probably a good thing for us consumers that bike companies have begun to settle on certain geo numbers and suspension designs. Maybe I'm niave but I think that it likely reflects that engineers have largely "figured out" mountain bike design (rather than an effort to pump out generic frames and drive profit). Bike design at performance MTB companies is supposedly an objective, data-driven process. Doesn't it make sense that -- absent some trademark patented design (like switch infinity, which -- while cool -- doesn't seem to elicit the same praise as it once did as rear shocks have improved) -- a bunch of smarty pants engineers would eventually start to conclude that a particular kind of design works best? Or would we be better off if companies continued to expiriment for the sake of it? Maybe, I don't know.

@ballsackington : keep in mind that seatpost insertion is measured how far a cylinder can go into the frame. on shock tunnel bikes the cable and actuator hit the tunnel's ceiling first, on a kinked seat tube they arent hitting anything. Same thing I said in their soft launch clip from last week: " I was an SC fan from ‘99-‘13 owning from 5 different models throughout that time period. They lost me when they first released the $10k Bronson as it didn’t ride notable better than similar bikes that cost notably less. I returned to SC ‘24 after being on a popular Horst-link that I just didn’t like. The modern lower-link VPP design was a stark contrast to how that bike pedaled and I remain a huge fan. I’m sure the latest TB will be fine, but it seems unfortunate to see the days when SC was leading the MTB world as innovator in the rear view mirror." However, watching Dario's spiel brought something else to mind. The original VPP design (Outland) was chasing the concept of making a full suspension bike pedal like a hardtail and in the nearly three decades since it arrived SC has slowly been rounding off it's sharp edges. These include pedal kick back and rear wheel hang up during high torque moments in rough terrain; the stuff that became a nuisance for the suspension platform. On the converse, small amounts of those make for an energetic ride if less calm. While the Horst-link design is widely popular, IMHO it mutes these features too much for trail bikes (great on a DH rig/e-bike and too much for XC as all of them went to flex pivots to regain that energy). I like the later generations of the VPP specifically b/c of this energy if not slightly chaotic feel. This makes for a bike that can take all the hits, but is more play than plow. Yes, I know that those are broad characterizations, but I plan to keep my 5010 MX going for the chaotic fun it delivers until something tops it.

I think with the front derailleurs gone, suspension design became more about main pivot placement and shock actuation. Less so much about how the seatstay and chainstay interact at the back of the bike. 99/100 XC bikes are now a single pivot with a linkage shock connection. Back when front derailleurs forced the main pivot to sit below nearer the bottom bracket forced all of the marketed designs. So this is all SRAMs! fault for phasing out front derailleurs!

@Spencermon : Ignore HeatedRotor, he tried to get me to hook up with him and rail him in the backseat of his car, but I blew him off because I am quite straight and married but apparently that doesn't sit well with him, so now he stalks me and replies to all my comments with troll posts in hoping to get my attention so he can have another chance (I wish I was kidding btw, Im not) For the technical stuff, anti squat, its quite real, the problem is that people tie it to a concept called pedal kickback (for tests, they drop the bike on the ground, and watch the pedal spin backwards, claiming that this is pedal kickback). When the wheel isn't spinning, the same chain stretch effect that causes anti squat work (chain pulling suspension into extension) also works in reverse (suspension compression pulling on the chain and thus the pedals). However there are many videos and articles, including Pinkbikes, that shows that its basically next to impossible for the pedal kickback to happen when riding downhill. The only time you feel it is if you are pedaling over rough stuff, because the drivetrain is under tension, so the wheel can affect the pedals. When descending, there is enough slack in the chain, and the wheel is spinning fast enough to where the freehub being rotated forward by the chain stretching has no way of catching up with the wheel. But yet, somehow people still claim that pedal kickback is a thing. Ochain devices work because they isolate chain and derailleur cage movement. But things like low engagement hubs specifically for pedal kickback are quite pointless.

>The Orbit Bar allows us to fine-tune the kinematics to partially isolate the axle path and pedal kickback values from the anti-squat characteristics. You can't isolate pedal kickback from anti squat. The nature of the chain pulling on the suspension to extend it for anti squat characteristics works in exact opposite way of the suspension pulling on the chain which pulls on the pedals.

@phops : @phops : The graphs tell a different story. I think that summary is the best way to explain it because the actual orbit bar system is a 4-bar separate from the shock linkage, allowing us to tune the kinematics without affecting every characteristic. There's a reason I used the word "partially." It’s not actually isolating it; it’s just allowing us to move the values farther apart.

@phops : the primary force in anti-squat comes from the weight transfer working against the geometry of the axle path / IC. the portion of anti-squat from chain tension is actually quite minimal. The IC of course affects chain growth so the two cannot be completely decoupled (except via eliminating the chain via shaft drive or a hub motor, for example - which will still have antisquat despite being chainless), but it's not like the antisquat curve is just the inverse of the chain growth curve. As Ethan points out, the geometry can be manipulated to make those two less (or more) correlated.

@phops : true for a given suspension and drive train layout. Some suspension designs have better anti squat to kickback ratios as others, its just a shame not many bikes have publishes Kickbakc vs travel graphs. The shape of the graphs also vary wildly. Just compare VPP, DW Link and a horstlink bike. The Arid Spade for example has an anti squat graph that looks like a mix between horstlink (which are primarily a straight line nwith not much if any curvature to it) and VPP (which starts high, stays high but in the second half falls of at a extrem pace an amount, strongly curved line). This wouldnt be possible on a standard horstlink or single pivot

@ohio : >he primary force in anti-squat comes from the weight transfer working against the geometry of the axle path / IC Unless you go high pivot, the chain line sort of follows the line from axle to IC, so the values are generally very related. Secondly, that part that you mention varies quite a bit. The CG of the rider+bike is not static. For example, when you stand up pedal, the weight transfer anti squat goes out the window. The chain based one does not.

@Kilian1b15Gt : The problem is that with standard bike constraints and low pivot designs with 32" rings, you can't really change one without the other. The computation for anti squat is determine by the combination of IC/CG location+geo and chain effects, while the pedal kickback is solely from chain effects. If you move your pivot lower than the outer edge of 32 teeth, you reduce pedal kickback to zero, but your anti squat also goes pro squat. And vice versa. The only way to separate them to have a high pivot bike with either concentric drive like Starling Sturn or Tora EVH or have the upper pulley outer edge right on the chainline. In that case, your anti squat is governed solely by the IC/CG and geo.

@NickBit : That is actally a bit part of why steel frames are relatively „sustainable“ compared to other materials: It‘s such a widely used and relatively easily recyclable material that a lot of stell is recycled material and not much is „fresh“ from raw ore. As far as rember my research from a few years ago that can‘t quite be said about Aluminium. With Aluminium frames, one of the largest contributers to the CO2-Footprint was actually the large amount of time the frames spend being „baked“ in ovens at high temperatures. It makes a huge difference if the electricty needed for that is being supplied by a coal power plant oder by renewable sources. I write that to reiterate: You can‘t even say „material A is always greener than material B“. There are so many variables that you can only give a rough average trend or decide on an individual basis…if you‘ve got the data to actually enable you to make such a decision.

Here's a bit of insight ISO4210 is the international standard that all bikes are tested to as a baseline. It was last revised in 2023. This test does actually require a bike to be very strong, the horizontal push/pull tests in particular are brutal. If you don't test to this standard and your bike fails with a customer on, your defence lawyer is going to have a hell of a time. It might also be hard to get a bike through customs into some countries or to sell in certain retailers without evidence you tested to or beyond ISO 4210. Kids bikes like these 24 and 26" bikes have to get tested to the same standard as an adults bike (that a 240lb man would ride). There is no lower international standard for kids/teenagers bikes. There is a standard ISO 8098 which is for like ~16" wheel kids bikes and below, but for a 20, 24 or 26" kids bikes it has to meet adults standards - and that's why a lot of kids bikes are super overbuilt!

@LemonadeMoney : no way - the impacts from the rear transfer to the handlebar when descending tech on a hardtail. The up-down movement of the rear wheel moves the handlebar forward and back, resulting in sharp hand impacts every time, even when standing. Only way to avoid it is to not ride tech, or ride it slowly.

I rode hardtails regularly until I moved to a rockier mountainous area. I tried here for years, but after moving to flat pedals the harshness just got worse. Flat landings, terrible. Rock gardens, awful. Technical climbs punishing. Yes, I can ride my trails on a hardtail, but the level of enjoyment was so much lower and the speeds so much slower. Back east on smoother flowier tracks, an HT was awesome, espeically with all the steep punchy out of the saddle climbs. But now that I winch up and plummet down, a hardtail feels like the wrong tool. I see a few around here, but they are pretty rare and I always respect those that can ride aggressively on them.

@rickybobby19 : "What is it about hardtails?" is a great question worth discussing. Exhibit A - look at the bike in this article.... It is perfect. There is not a full suspension bike that will ever hold a candle to the aesthetics of a hardtail. Hardtails offer the raw perception of pure BIKE. The newest Specialized Epic 9 is like a 2026 Porsche 911. Metal hardtails are like a Shelby Cobra. Some people love their 911.... But let's be honest.... The Cobra has soul. Maybe you wouldn't take the Cobra on a 10 hour road trip, but you sure would rip it on a perfect afternoon. As far as the ability of a hardtail - I absolutely love ripping the single track flow on my hardtail, where I absolutely hated riding my full suspension on those same trails. Because these types of trails are my "home trail", I need a hardtail. I can sneak the hardtail down some pretty wild rock rolls, or session a ledge step-up here & there, but SUSTAINED and ROCKY descending is brutal. Two days ago I had to finish a sustained descent over jarring rocks on the hardtail and it was not fun for my spine. When I was riding a fully, I always envied the people riding hardtails. Now that I'm on a hardtail full time, I've never once craved a full suspension bike. The only thing about full suspension that entices me is the ability to trek a little bit longer, and to sustain energy better over long descents.... But I can rock out 45 miles of dirt on my hardtail if I select the correct trails, so it's great for me. I love hardtails.

In my case, I’ve felt like I needed to take higher risks on a full suspension trail bike to “feel something” on a trail. I tried a hardtail and out of all sudden all of those more mellow trails came to life, and got a lot more enjoyment out of them. Also they are more efficient to pedal, and I guess to get to the trails you don’t have to suffer as much. Also flatter trails become much more enjoyable in case you don’t have as much elevation. As a side bonus it makes you think about the lines a lot more, and riding feels less about chasing the fastest time, but more about the enjoyment of riding. Plus after you finish the trail, you have sort of a sense of accomplishment of “I just did that… on a hardtail!”. Just my two cents.

@Compositepro : No, sorry. It's all in German. But here's a quick summary: Commenters found out that the frame has striking resemblance to LightCarbon's LCFS947, which is an open mould frame that anyone can buy directly off LightCarbon or from importers for super cheap. Commenters then, amongst other things, started to call SCIU out for trying to sell an open mould frame for a ridiculous price. Once the marketing people realized that they had been found out, they started to make dubious claims about how the frame isn't actually open mould and that they vastly improved it by utilizing "superior raw materials" and having the carbon lay-up changed - but never followed up with anything to lend any credibility to their story. On top of that, they failed to provide any credible details about their plans to "offset" their carbon footprint and the other apparent steps towards ecological sustainability mentioned in their ad. So people naturally assumed that it was all just talk to white- or respectively green-wash their operation and to gain a favourable public opinion with no intentions to ever follow up on their claims. Another thing was that people researched the two founders of the company and as it turns out, neither has any sort of professional background in mechanical engineering, material science or similar. What they do have instead is a professional background in marketing. Most people assumed, that the latter tells you all you need to know about the company. SCIU was even given a chance by the MTB-News editorial to clarify things and calm the situation and repair some of the damage they had done to their own reputation. But instead of taking their chance, SCIU just published a nebulous statement comprised of blank, meaningless marketing jargon, insulted and ridiculed commenters and even ended up accusing the editorial team at MTB-News of lying. It's downright astonishing how much some people can talk without actually saying anything. Anyways, the whole thing was a right toss up and at this point, they don't deserve any better IMO.

I didn't follow the topic on mtb news (or any topic since a couple of years actually) but it was instantly obvious that this is some bs right there. I work as a climate consultant and while it's difficult to talk about their emission numbers without any calculations, basing their claims solely on compensation is a big no go. Compensation is at the very best (and that's still naive) a short-term solution and claims without any actual reduction plans and goals like science based targets should be discredited. Talking about choosing carbon fibre because of longevity is also incredibly stupid, because bicycle frames don't fail because of repeated load cycles, but because of crashes and other impacts to the material. The claim of better energy consumption of carbon fibre is also useless without specifying the materials in question...primary aluminium is known for having a bad cumulative energy demand but secondary aluminium is far far better in terms of energy usage, so you can bend the results easily to favour your process. Furthermore, reforestation programs are questionable because human-made forests often can't develop long-term and limit biodiversity, while doing nothing to open areas can yield much better results. Protecting or restoring wetlands has more potential to limit emissions.

@v7fmp : The similarity between these guys and Sick is that both are disingenuous, but in quite different ways. Sick were making interesting frames (some of their designs were wild AF) but they were definitely their own designs. They were then selling bikes based on CAD renderings that they actually didn't actually have the capacity, or the funds, to deliver. So Sick had their own designs, but your frame might now turn up - and if it does it doesn't seem like it was thoroughly prototyped and developed. This company will certainly be able to deliver because it's an open mould factory frame - there's probably hundreds of frames already made that just need a lick of paint and some decals putting on them and they're likely good to go. The bikes are probably decent enough and work well, but whether or not the prices they're charging for them are fair or good value is a completely different discussion

Also worth mentioning @Ghostifari - when you clearcut a forest, loads of Co2 is emitted into atmposhpere from rotting ground and biomass left overs. Wood needs processing: drying is very energy heavy. So what you have after you have cut down a forest or tree plantation is a carbon pile worth of up to 20 years of sequestration! That means that a new tree plantation will start net sequestration earliest after 20 years and then it is cut at 40 (if it doesn't die due to forest fire or bark beetle) which means almost nothing changes. Not to mention that a tree plantation is not as good at sequestration as an actual forest, is more prone to fire and pests. So what all these basterds are counting is a prognosis of how much carbon a tree can sequester, and timber industry rarely mentions how much their operation sends to the atmosphere - finally nail to the coffin - cradle to grave - they all count how long a wood product is prognosed to last, be recycled and disposed in the end. This is the second article I’ve read here this week that says there is a link between frame stiffness and swings in traction large enough for a rider busy mountain biking to notice. Anyone care to substantiate this in any way? I have good library access and will get the paper from behind the paywall if you can cite a source. Happy to share it here. Tia

Anecdotally I've run into problems with overly stiff frames (and wheels as well) in terms of cornering traction. That could just be me, though. On paper this makes sense, since the front and rear wheels don't track each other in many situations so some frame flex should help. In practice, I'm a bit dubious that it's a real thing.

I doubt there's anything published about bicycles, but it's a well known phenomenon in motogp and racing motorcycles. They are an extreme example, but the theory is that when you corner a 2 wheeled vehicle, you lean it. Once you lean it, the bump input isn't acting in line with the suspension, so that becomes less effective at keeping the wheels on the ground and maintaining traction. By having some flex along the length of the bike you allow the chassis to respond to some of these bump inputs when leant over. I call it 'breathing with the trail'. It's a little hippish, but it's the way it feels to me.

@cotic-bikes : that really does help me understand, alongside what @waltworks said, thank you. My first naive thought really was in a wheel, suspension, and frame perpendicular to the ground orientation. Imagining what a test rig would look like bit not considering lean

Beautiful. But it has only 249mm of seatpost insertion depth in size C3 (482mm reach - size L) despite its 444mm long seat tube. Goddamnit. It's almost 2024 and bike companies are still doing this!!! I mean, a decent dropper with 185mm of travel (which usually is considered a perfect fit for a frame size L) needs around 290mm of insertion depth. This means if the insertion depth is restricted to around 250mm, the seat tube actually could as well be 40mm longer - which would be a 485mm long seat tube in this case. WTF - when will they finally get it right.

Depends a little on your leg length. The crimson bike in the photos has a 160mm Bikeyoke dropper and that isn't fully inserted and no one who tried it had an issue with saddle height. That would suggest to me that a 180mm One Up would go in no problem for most riders looking at this size. Seat post insertion vs pivot location is a tricky compromise to balance. It much more difficult/expensive/heavier to have a pivot dangling off the front of the seat tube, and it impacts the link lengths, kinematic etc. It's not that we haven't considered it, but all manufacturing is a compromise and this is where we are striking ours at the moment.

@cotic-bikes : Thanks for the reply and especially thanks for specifing insertion depths in the first place. Most companies don't even do that. It is clear to me that compromises have to be made when designing a frame - or whatever else. One Ups required insertion depth is particularly short - which helps of course. But still it would stick out 2cm when bottoming out inside the seat tube. Funny thing is: Your pivot location design choice actually means the seat tube on size C3 could well be about 3cm longer without compromising anything any further. But probably then people wouldn't buy it because of the long seat tube...haha. Btw: I am 1,84cm (6') tall with a inseam height of 81cm. My frame needs to have 480mm reach, and a 445mm seat tube with full 290mm of insterion depth so everything works out fine. Maybe not the norm - though 445mm seat tubes in size L are...so what for if not for shorter people or those with short legs?

@mtb-daniel : No problem. It's always interesting to get feedback. The shorter seat tubes are more for shorter legged riders at the other end of the fit scale who might not otherwise get a 150 or 160mm dropper in. To be fair, most people your height have slightly longer legs, and we would usually recommend a C4 size as well. That's just our thoughts on it though. If you like 480mm reach then all good.

@ScandiumRider : I reduced the number of flights I take from 2-3 per week, to 1 per quarter. I drive used cars. I recycle, I don't use single use plastics, and I teach my kids to take care of our planet. But I will never, ever, ever give a shit about the carbon foot print of my bike. Bicycles have been the only consistent joy in my life since I was a child, and fretting about the carbon footprint required to manufacture it reduces the joy one has whilst riding it. It's also a useless metric, as having a bicycle massively offsets carbon emissions, as I often choose to pedal 10 miles to the trail head rather than drive. If we have a new material in ten years that is universally better in every way, sure -- I'll try it. But I just don't give a shit. I want a bike that I like to look at with a rate of progression appropriate for my trails, a good axle path, and a unique story to tell about it. @Dougal-SC : Automation and mechanisation literally does replace people. That's been one of the main benefits all along, alongside efficiency, both for the reason capitalists can extract more profit as they a) don't have to pay the wages of living human beings who (unlike machines) can only work a certain amount of hours per day and b) they can produce higher volumes of goods. Some human input will always be required, so wages are unavoidable, however if you outsource that labour to south East Asia, you can minimise those costs and increase profits. Through reinvesting some of those profits into production (machines/ technology) to maintain competitiveness, over many years yes we have arrived at a point where automation can produce cheap goods, like clothes quickly and efficiently, as you say. That said, many of these goods are of poor quality and are not built to last. In the USA it's something like every garment is only worn an average of 6 times before going to landfill. The labour used in these factories, for example in Bangladesh, is basically slavery and the environmental cost of production and disposal is huge. Is this something to be celebrated? Whatever the case, thanks to this process and despite it's harm, we have the technology to produce high quality clothes (and other goods) that last. But we don't...why is that? Because the investment required to do so is too high and the profit margins are too slim compared to the maintenance of the current rotten system and continued profit extraction. The irony now is that through this process, production costs (which includes wages) in South East Asia have risen as they have become more skilled. And as we have deindustrialised in the name of profit in the global north and west, the cost of producing these goods back in the Europe or the US is immense. Hence the current economic crisis. So what do capitalists do to get around this? If they want to bring production back to Europe, it's going to cost. And the capitalists sure as hell aren't going to foot the bill, it's the workers/ customers who will. But how do the capitalists brand and advertise this increase in costs? "As an analogy, it's like reclaiming timber from a demolished building - you're not going to build a house of the same size and strength as the original," That's a very wrong analogy, because you certainly could build a house of comparable _strength_ with reclaimed timber, and especially so with things like LVL and OSB. It might cost more, might might be heavier (less concern for a house than a bike, but also not a given for the house), and might also need a different structure, but then end result isn't going be radically weaker.
I think if you discount weight (like you’re doing here) you could certainly also build a strong enough mtb frame with the reclaimed fibres. I don‘t think the analogy is that bad

@lejake : a) the analogy ignored weight, b) as I said it's not a given that the reclaimed house will be heavier: something like LVL is very precisely engineered and often is used in place of solid beams of much greater size and weight. It's why I put two "might"s in there, because it's far from a given but there is a chance and I just wanted to cover the bases.
